symbols: reed cross with banner — John's proclamation of the coming Christ
lamb at his feet — the Lamb of God whom John identified and proclaimed
bare muscular torso — the ascetic life in the wilderness
draped cloth — the rough garment of camel's hair described in the Gospels
upward-pointing gesture — directing attention away from himself toward the one who comes after
banner reading text — the Agnus Dei proclamation
description: A muscular, bearded man with wild dark hair sits in a dramatic pose against a dark background with hints of wilderness. His upper body is bare, with rough cloth draped around his waist and legs. He holds a tall staff topped with a small cross and a banner. One hand gestures upward or toward his chest. A small lamb lies at his feet in the lower portion. The painting is dark and dramatic with strong chiaroscuro, the figure emerging powerfully from the shadows.
theological_description: John the Baptist is the last and greatest of the Old Testament prophets and the immediate forerunner of Christ. All four Gospels identify him as the voice crying in the wilderness foretold by Isaiah: 'Prepare the way of the Lord' (ISA.40.3; MAT.3.3). His preaching called Israel to repentance and baptism in preparation for the coming Kingdom. John's supreme prophetic moment came when he identified Jesus: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (JHN.1.29). The cross-staff he holds is an iconographic convention pointing to both his proclamation and his martyrdom. Jesus himself testified: 'Among those born of women there has arisen no one greater than John the Baptist' (MAT.11.11). John's entire ministry was one of self-emptying: 'He must increase, but I must decrease' (JHN.3.30). The lamb at his feet makes his Christological proclamation visible — he is the prophet whose sole purpose was to point beyond himself to the Paschal Lamb.
